stem: A 22-year-old client with a severe traumatic brain injury is unresponsive with fixed, dilated pupils. The healthcare team is initiating the brain death protocol. Based on the EHR, select the potential condition, two actions to take, and two parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
Situation: At 07:00, we are initiating the brain death determination protocol for the 22-year-old male in ICU Bed 4.
Background: He sustained a severe diffuse axonal injury from a motor vehicle accident 48 hours ago. He is ventilator and pressor-dependent.
Assessment: GCS is 3T. Pupils are fixed and dilated. Corneal, gag, and oculocephalic reflexes are absent. He is poikilothermic with a core temp of 35.1°C (95.2°F). No spontaneous respirations over the ventilator.
Recommendation: Continue protocol. Neurology has ordered a formal apnea test once normothermia is achieved. Notify the Organ Procurement Organization (OPO) per policy.

tabs.title: Vital Signs
tabs.content:

Notes: Client is dependent on norepinephrine infusion to maintain MAP > 65 mmHg.

tabs.content:

tabs.title: Brain Death Protocol
tabs.content:
CriteriaCurrent FindingInterpretation
Pupillary Light ReflexAbsentConsistent with brainstem failure
Corneal / Gag ReflexesAbsentConsistent with brainstem failure
Oculocephalic ReflexAbsentConsistent with brainstem failure
Apnea TestPending - Awaiting normothermia (Core Temp > 36°C)Required for final diagnosis

conditionOptions.hoverRationale: Incorrect. PVS involves loss of higher cortical function but preserved brainstem function, including spontaneous respirations and sleep-wake cycles. This client has absent brainstem reflexes and apnea.
Correct. This condition is the irreversible cessation of all brain function, including the brainstem. The client's absent reflexes, apnea, and coma are hallmark signs of this diagnosis.
Incorrect. While a drug-induced coma can mimic brain death, it is a reversible condition. A toxicology screen would be performed to rule this out before a brain death diagnosis is confirmed.

actionOptions.hoverRationale: Correct. Federal law and hospital policy mandate timely notification to the OPO when a client meets clinical triggers for brain death. This allows the OPO to prepare for potential organ donation discussions.

actionOptions.text: Maintain hemodynamic stability for organ perfusion
actionOptions.hoverRationale: Correct. After brain death, hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction leads to hemodynamic instability. Maintaining MAP and organ perfusion with fluids and vasopressors is critical to preserve organ viability for potential donation.

actionOptions.text: Explain ventilator function to the family
actionOptions.hoverRationale: Incorrect. While family communication is vital, the priority actions are fulfilling legal requirements (OPO notification) and preserving the option of organ donation. This explanation may also be misleading if not carefully worded.
Incorrect. Passive range-of-motion is not a priority. The focus shifts from restorative care to maintaining physiologic stability for organ procurement or compassionate withdrawal of support.

parameterOptions.text: Absence of brainstem reflexes and spontaneous respirations
parameterOptions.hoverRationale: Correct. The core of the clinical diagnosis of brain death is the documented, irreversible absence of all brainstem reflexes and the inability to breathe spontaneously, confirmed by an apnea test.

parameterOptions.text: Confirm normothermic core temperature for testing
parameterOptions.hoverRationale: Correct. Hypothermia can depress CNS function and mimic brain death. A core temperature above 36°C is required to ensure that the absence of neurological function is not due to a reversible metabolic cause.
Incorrect. While glucose monitoring is part of general critical care, it is not a specific parameter used to confirm or deny a diagnosis of brain death. The key parameters are neurological.
Incorrect. Trousseau's sign indicates hypocalcemia and neuromuscular irritability. It is irrelevant to the diagnosis of brain death, which focuses on the absence of brainstem function.
